grammarLessons.name: Subjunctive Intro — espero que, quiero que

cards.grammarNote:
Adverb form: **felizmente** (happily) — add **-mente** to the feminine adjective
**feliz** has no gender change: feliz + mente → **felizmente** · **Ella sonrió felizmente.**
Adverb form: **tristemente** (sadly) — **triste** has no gender change: triste + mente → **tristemente** · **Habló tristemente.**

**amor** comes from Latin **amor** (love, desire) — the same root as English *amorous*.
Verb forms: **amar** (to love deeply), **querer** (to love/want — more everyday).
**Te quiero** (I love you — common between family/friends) vs **Te amo** (I love you — more intense/romantic).
cards.englishPhrase: I feel a lot of love for my family.
cards.phrase: Siento mucho amor por mi familia.

cards.grammarNote:
**ira** (anger, wrath) is a strong, formal word — from Latin **ira**.
Everyday equivalents: **enojo** (anger, annoyance) and **enfado** (more Spain).
To express becoming angry: **ponerse furioso** or **enojarse** (reflexive) — **me enojé** (I got angry).
cards.englishPhrase: I felt anger when they lied to me.

cards.grammarNote:
**tener miedo** = to be afraid (literally *to have fear*) — Spanish uses **tener** + noun for many states.
**tener miedo de/a + infinitive**: **tengo miedo de caer** (I'm afraid of falling).
Also: **dar miedo** = to frighten — **me da miedo** (it frightens me / it scares me).

**esperanza** (hope) is a noun; the verb is **esperar** (to hope, to wait, to expect).
Note the suffix **-anza** forming abstract nouns: **confianza** (trust), **enseñanza** (teaching), **esperanza** (hope).
**Espero que + subjunctive**: **espero que todo salga bien** (I hope everything goes well).
cards.englishPhrase: I have hope for the future.

cards.grammarNote:
**estrés** is a borrowed word from English *stress* — note the accent on the final syllable.
The adjective is **estresado/a** (stressed): **estoy estresado** (I'm stressed) — uses **estar** for the temporary state.
Also: **estresante** (stressful) — **un trabajo estresante** (a stressful job).
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of stress at work.
cards.phrase: Tengo mucho estrés en el trabajo.

cards.grammarNote:
**estado de ánimo** = mood (literally *state of spirit/soul*).
**ánimo** alone means spirit, cheer, courage — **¡Ánimo!** = Cheer up! / Come on!
Also common: **estar de buen humor** (to be in a good mood) and **estar de mal humor** (to be in a bad mood).
cards.englishPhrase: I'm in a good mood today.
cards.phrase: Hoy estoy de buen estado de ánimo.

cards.grammarNote:
**sentimiento** (feeling, emotion) comes from **sentir** (to feel).
The suffix **-miento** forms nouns from verbs: **sentir → sentimiento**, **conocer → conocimiento** (knowledge), **pensar → pensamiento** (thought).
Also related: **sentido** = sense (as in the five senses, or *meaning*).

**emoción** = emotion, but also *excitement* — context determines the meaning.
The verb **emocionar** = to move emotionally / to excite: **me emociona** (it moves me / it excites me).
Reflexive **emocionarse** = to get emotional: **se emocionó** (he/she got emotional / teared up).

**calma** (noun) = calm, calmness.
Adjective form: **tranquilo/a** or **calmado/a** (calm, relaxed).
Use **estar** for the temporary state: **estoy tranquilo** (I'm calm right now) vs **soy tranquilo** (I'm a calm person by nature).
Also: **mantener la calma** (to keep calm) — a very common phrase.

**paz** (peace) — from Latin **pax** (same root as English *pacify*, *pacific*).
Note the plural: **paces** (though rarely used). The phrase **hacer las paces** = to make peace / to make up.
**tranquilidad** (quiet, tranquility) is a close synonym — **necesito paz y tranquilidad** (I need peace and quiet).

**preocupación** (worry, concern) — noun from **preocupar** (to worry/concern).
The reflexive **preocuparse** = to worry oneself: **me preocupo** (I worry), **no te preocupes** (don't worry).
The suffix **-ción** forms feminine nouns from verbs: **preocupar → preocupación**, **emocionar → emoción**.
cards.phrase: No dejes que la preocupación te detenga.

cards.grammarNote:
**sorpresa** (surprise) — from **sorprender** (to surprise).
**¡Qué sorpresa!** = What a surprise! — the **¡Qué + noun!** structure expresses exclamation.
Adjective: **sorprendido/a** (surprised) — use **estar**: **estoy sorprendido** (I'm surprised).
Also: **me sorprendió** (it surprised me) — verb used like **gustar** with indirect object pronouns.

**pasión** (passion) — from Latin **passio** (suffering, strong feeling), same root as English *passion*.
Adjective: **apasionado/a** (passionate, passionate about): **es apasionado por la música** (he's passionate about music).
**con pasión** = with passion — preposition **con** + noun is common for manner: **habla con entusiasmo** (he speaks with enthusiasm).

**deseo** (desire, wish) — noun from **desear** (to desire, to wish).
**tener deseo de + infinitive** = to have a desire to do something: **tengo un gran deseo de viajar**.
Also: **desear** = to wish — **te deseo lo mejor** (I wish you the best).
cards.englishPhrase: I have a strong desire to travel.
cards.phrase: Tengo un gran deseo de viajar.

cards.grammarNote:
**odio** = hate (noun) — also the yo form of **odiar** (to hate): **yo odio** (I hate).
Note that **odio** as a noun takes a definite article: **el odio es destructivo** (hate is destructive).
Compare: **ira** (wrath/anger — more formal), **rencor** (resentment, grudge), **enojo** (everyday anger).
cards.phrase: El odio es un sentimiento fuerte.

cards.grammarNote:
**placer** (pleasure) — from Latin **placere** (to please), same root as English *placid*, *please*.
**Es un placer** = It's a pleasure — used as a polite greeting when meeting someone.
**con placer** = with pleasure — a polite response to a request.
Related: **agradable** (pleasant, agreeable) — **una experiencia agradable**.
cards.englishPhrase: It's a pleasure to meet you.

cards.grammarNote:
**dolor** (pain) — from Latin **dolor**, same root as English *dolorous*.
**tener dolor de + body part**: **tengo dolor de cabeza** (I have a headache), **tengo dolor de estómago** (I have a stomachache).
Verb: **doler** (to hurt/ache) — works like **gustar**: **me duele la cabeza** (my head hurts — lit. the head hurts me).
cards.englishPhrase: I feel pain in my leg.

cards.grammarNote:
**energía** (energy) — note the accent on the **í** (énfasis on the third syllable from the end).
**tener energía** = to have energy — using **tener** for states: **tengo mucha energía** (I have a lot of energy).
Adjective: **enérgico/a** (energetic) — use **ser** for a personality trait: **es muy enérgico** (he's very energetic).
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of energy today.

cards.grammarNote:
**alivio** (relief) — from **aliviar** (to relieve, to ease).
**¡Qué alivio!** = What a relief! — the **¡Qué + noun!** pattern expresses strong emotion.
**suspirar de alivio** = to sigh with relief — a very natural collocation.
Also: **aliviar el dolor** (to relieve pain), **me alivió saber eso** (it was a relief to know that).

**consuelo** (comfort, consolation) — from **consolar** (to console, to comfort).
Note: **consuelo** implies emotional comfort after grief or hardship — more specific than English *comfort*.
For physical comfort, use **comodidad**: **este sofá es muy cómodo** (this sofa is very comfortable).
Phrase: **ser un consuelo** = to be a comfort: **sus palabras fueron un consuelo** (her words were a comfort).

**sentir** = to feel/perceive something · **sentirse** (reflexive) = to feel a certain way
**Me siento bien** (I feel good) · **Siento dolor** (I feel pain)

**amar** = to love (deeply, often romantic or profound love).
Compare **querer** = to love/want — more versatile and common in everyday speech: **te quiero** (I love you) is used between family and close friends.
**Te amo** is more intense and typically romantic; **te quiero** is warmer and more common day-to-day.

**odiar** = to hate — a regular -ar verb: **odio, odias, odia, odiamos, odiáis, odian**.
**odiar + infinitive** = to hate doing something: **odio esperar** (I hate waiting), **odia llegar tarde** (he hates being late).
Less intense alternatives: **no soportar** (can't stand), **detestar** (to detest).

**gustar** works opposite to English — the subject is what is liked, not the person.
**Me gusta la música** = Music pleases me (I like music).
Indirect object pronouns: **me** (to me), **te** (to you), **le** (to him/her), **nos** (to us), **les** (to them).
**Me gustan** (plural) vs **me gusta** (singular): **me gustan los libros** (I like books).

**disfrutar** = to enjoy — can take **de** or a direct object: **disfrutar de la vida** or **disfrutar la vida**.
Both are correct; **disfrutar de** is slightly more formal.
Also reflexive: **disfrutarse** is used in some Latin American countries — **¡disfrútalo!** (enjoy it!).

**temer** = to fear (formal/literary) — a regular -er verb.
More colloquial: **tener miedo de** (to be afraid of) — **tengo miedo de lo desconocido** (I'm afraid of the unknown).
**temer que + subjunctive** = to fear that: **temo que sea demasiado tarde** (I fear it may be too late).
Note: **lo peor** = the worst (neuter article **lo** + adjective forms a noun phrase).

**esperar** has three meanings: to hope, to wait, and to expect.
**esperar que + subjunctive** = to hope that: **espero que todo salga bien** (I hope everything goes well).
**esperar a + infinitive** = to wait to do something: **espero a ver** (I'll wait and see).
Context clarifies which meaning is intended — often all three blend together.

**preocuparse** is a reflexive verb — to worry oneself.
Full conjugation: **me preocupo, te preocupas, se preocupa, nos preocupamos**.
**No te preocupes** = Don't worry — the **te** is the reflexive pronoun (tú form).
Non-reflexive **preocupar** = to worry someone else: **me preocupa** (it worries me / I'm worried about it).

**calmar** = to calm (someone else) — transitive: **calmé a mi hermana** (I calmed my sister).
Reflexive **calmarse** = to calm down: **cálmate** (calm down! — tú command).
Similar: **tranquilizarse** (to calm down), **relajarse** (to relax) — all reflexive for self-directed action.
**Cálmate** in the example is an affirmative tú command formed by dropping the **-s** from **te calmas**.

**consolar** = to console, to comfort — a stem-changing verb (o → ue): **consuelo, consuelas, consuela**.
The personal **a** is required before direct object people: **consoló a su amiga** (she comforted her friend).
Noun form: **consuelo** (consolation, comfort) — **fue un gran consuelo** (it was a great comfort).

**emocionar** = to excite, to move emotionally — works like **gustar**: the thing that excites is the subject.
**El viaje me emociona** = The trip excites me (lit. the trip moves me).
Reflexive **emocionarse** = to get excited / to become emotional: **me emociono cuando escucho esa canción** (I get emotional when I hear that song).
Note: **emocionado/a** (excited/moved) uses **estar** — a temporary state.

**molestar** = to bother, to annoy — works like **gustar**: the annoying thing is the subject.
**Ese ruido me molesta** = That noise bothers me (lit. that noise molests/disturbs me).
Reflexive **molestarse** = to be bothered / to take the trouble: **no te molestes** (don't bother).
Note: **molestar** is not as strong as English *molest* — it simply means to disturb or annoy.
**¡Qué vergüenza!** = How embarrassing! / What a shame! · **vergüenza** can mean both embarrassment and shame
**celos** is typically plural · **tener celos** = to be jealous · **envidia** = envy (wanting what someone else has)

**irritar** = to irritate, to annoy — works like **gustar** and **molestar**: the irritating thing is the subject.
**Esperar me irrita** = Waiting irritates me (lit. waiting irritates me).
Reflexive **irritarse** = to get irritated: **se irrita con facilidad** (he gets irritated easily).
Adjective: **irritante** (irritating) — **es muy irritante** (it's very irritating).

**enojar** = to anger (someone else) — transitive: **ese comentario me enojó** (that comment angered me).
Reflexive **enojarse** = to get angry: **me enojé** (I got angry), **se enoja fácilmente** (he gets angry easily).
**ponerse enojado/a** = to become angry — **se puso enojada** (she became angry).
Note: **enojado** uses **estar** — **estoy enojado** (I'm angry right now).

**deleitar** = to delight — works like **gustar**: the delightful thing is the subject.
**La música deleitó a todos** = The music delighted everyone — note the personal **a** before **todos** (people as direct object).
Noun form: **deleite** (delight, pleasure) — **fue un deleite** (it was a delight).
Reflexive **deleitarse en/con** = to delight in: **se deleita en leer** (he delights in reading).

**sonreír** = to smile — a compound of **son-** (sound/tone) + **reír** (to laugh).
**sonreír** is an irregular e→i stem-changing verb: **sonrío, sonríes, sonríe, sonreímos, sonreís, sonríen**.
Noun: **la sonrisa** (the smile) — **tiene una sonrisa hermosa** (she has a beautiful smile).
Note the written accent on **sonríe** to separate the two vowels.

**reír** = to laugh — irregular e→i stem-changing verb: **río, ríes, ríe, reímos, reís, ríen**.
Reflexive **reírse** is also common and essentially interchangeable: **nos reímos** (we laughed).
**reírse de** = to laugh at: **se ríen de mí** (they're laughing at me).
Noun: **la risa** (the laugh, laughter) — **me dio risa** (it made me laugh).

**llorar** = to cry — a regular -ar verb: **lloro, lloras, llora, lloramos, lloráis, lloran**.
The present progressive **está llorando** (is crying) uses **estar** + gerund (-ando/-iendo).
**hacerse llorar** = to make someone cry: **me hizo llorar** (it made me cry).
Note: **llover** (to rain) sounds similar but is unrelated — **llueve** (it's raining).

**suspirar** = to sigh — a regular -ar verb.
**suspirar de alivio** = to sigh with/of relief — **de** links the action to its emotional cause.
Noun: **el suspiro** (the sigh) — **lanzó un suspiro** (he let out a sigh), **un suspiro de alivio** (a sigh of relief).
Also figurative: **en un suspiro** = in the blink of an eye / in no time at all.

**fruncir el ceño** = to frown (literally *to furrow/wrinkle the brow*).
**fruncir** = to wrinkle, to knit — **fruncir los labios** (to purse one's lips).
**el ceño** = the brow/furrowed expression — not to be confused with **la ceja** (eyebrow).
Past tense: **frunció el ceño** — regular -ir verb in the preterite, third person singular: **frunció**.
cards.englishPhrase: He frowned when he heard the news.
cards.phrase: Frunció el ceño cuando escuchó la noticia.

cards.grammarNote:
**gritar** = to shout, to scream — a regular -ar verb.
**No grites** = Don't shout — negative tú command: use the present subjunctive (**grites**).
Noun: **el grito** (the shout, scream) — **un grito de alegría** (a cry of joy), **a voces / a gritos** (shouting, at the top of one's voice).
Also: **dar un grito** = to let out a shout.

**relajarse** is a reflexive verb — to relax (oneself).
**Relájate** = Relax! — affirmative tú command of the reflexive: drop **-s** from **te relajas**, then attach **-te**: **relájate** (note the accent to preserve stress).
Full conjugation: **me relajo, te relajas, se relaja, nos relajamos**.
Also: **estar relajado/a** = to be relaxed (state) — **estoy muy relajado** (I'm very relaxed).
Adverb form: **enojadamente** (angrily) — feminine: enojada + mente → **enojadamente** · **Respondió enojadamente.**

**enfadado** = mad, angry — more common in Spain; **enojado** is preferred in Latin America.
Both use **estar** for a temporary emotional state: **estoy enfadado** (I'm mad right now).
Verb: **enfadarse** (reflexive) = to get angry: **me enfado** (I get angry), **se enfadó** (he/she got angry).
**ponerse enfadado** = to become mad — **se puso enfadada** (she got angry).

**asustado/a** = scared, frightened — past participle of **asustar** (to frighten) used as an adjective.
Always use **estar**: **estaba asustado** (he was scared) — a temporary emotional state.
Verb: **asustarse** (reflexive) = to get scared: **me asusté** (I got scared), **no te asustes** (don't get scared).
Also: **tener miedo** (to be afraid) and **asustado** often overlap — **tenía miedo** vs **estaba asustado**.
Adverb form: **ansiosamente** (anxiously) — feminine: ansiosa + mente → **ansiosamente** · **Esperaba ansiosamente.**
Adverb form: **nerviosamente** (nervously) — feminine: nerviosa + mente → **nerviosamente** · **Habló nerviosamente.**
Adverb form: **emocionadamente** (excitedly) — feminine: emocionada + mente → **emocionadamente** · **Gritó emocionadamente.**

**alegre** (joyful, cheerful) — from Latin **alacer** (lively, brisk), the same root as **alegría** (joy).
The suffix **-ía** forms abstract nouns: **alegre → alegría** (joy), **cortés → cortesía** (courtesy).
Note the use of **estar**: **siempre está alegre** (she's always cheerful) — even with *siempre*, **estar** is used because it describes a perceived/observed state.
**ponerse alegre** = to become cheerful/tipsy — **me pongo alegre con el vino** (wine makes me happy/tipsy).
Adverb form: **orgullosamente** (proudly) — feminine: orgullosa + mente → **orgullosamente** · **Sonrió orgullosamente.**

**contento/a** = content, happy, pleased — always use **estar**: **estoy contento** (I'm content/pleased).
Do not confuse with **contento** as a noun (rarely used) or **contenido** (content, as in the content of a book).
**sentirse contento** = to feel content: **me siento contento con mi vida** (I feel content with my life).
Related: **satisfecho/a** (satisfied) — **estoy satisfecho** (I'm satisfied).
Adverb form: **tranquilamente** (calmly) — feminine: tranquila + mente → **tranquilamente** · **Respondió tranquilamente.**

**estresado/a** = stressed — past participle of **estresar** used as an adjective.
Always use **estar**: **estoy muy estresado** (I'm very stressed) — a temporary state, not a permanent personality trait.
**ponerse estresado** = to become stressed: **me pongo estresado cuando hay mucho trabajo** (I get stressed when there's a lot of work).
The noun **estrés** is a loanword from English — note the accent on the final syllable.

**abrumado/a** = overwhelmed — from **abrumar** (to overwhelm, to weigh down).
**sentirse abrumado** uses the reflexive **sentirse**: **me siento abrumado** (I feel overwhelmed).
Use **estar** for the resulting state: **estoy abrumado** (I'm overwhelmed).
The preposition **por** marks the cause: **abrumado por el trabajo** (overwhelmed by the work).
cards.englishPhrase: I feel overwhelmed by the work.
cards.phrase: Me siento abrumado por el trabajo.

cards.grammarNote:
**energético/a** = energetic — adjective from **energía** (energy).
Use **ser** for a personality trait: **es muy energética** (she's very energetic by nature).
Contrast with **estar lleno/a de energía** (to be full of energy — a current state): **hoy estoy lleno de energía**.
Note gender agreement: **energético** (masculine), **energética** (feminine).
Adverb form: **cansadamente** (wearily) — feminine: cansada + mente → **cansadamente** · **Arrastró los pies cansadamente.**

**agotado/a** = exhausted, depleted — from **agotar** (to exhaust, to drain, to use up).
Always use **estar**: **estoy agotado** (I'm exhausted) — a temporary physical/emotional state.
**agotado** can also mean *sold out*: **las entradas están agotadas** (the tickets are sold out) — same verb, different context.
**agotador/a** = exhausting: **fue un día agotador** (it was an exhausting day).

**curioso/a** = curious — can describe a person or a thing (meaning *strange/interesting*).
Use **estar** for the current emotional state: **estoy curioso por eso** (I'm curious about that).
Use **ser** for personality: **es una persona muy curiosa** (she's a very curious person).
Also: **tener curiosidad** = to be curious: **tengo mucha curiosidad por saber** (I'm very curious to know).
